results.possibleIdentity: Burt Webb is a multi-disciplinary technologist, science researcher, and media artist based in Seattle, Washington, who has been active since the early 1970s. His work frequently explores the intersection of science, technology, and consciousness, aligning with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1973, Webb co-founded the Seattle-based think tank Organization for the Advancement of Knowledge (OAK) with Richard Alan Miller. That same year, he co-authored a pioneering paper titled "The Holographic Concept of Reality," which was presented at the 1st Psychotronic Conference in Prague and explored the connection between holography and human consciousness. Webb is also known for inspiring and starring in the 1973-1974 cult classic science fiction short film "Eat the Sun," a documentary-style spoof about a religion based on electronic biofeedback. His artistic and technical contributions extend to major media, including providing computer graphics for the 1984 film "Star Trek III: The Search for Spock." Throughout his career, he has served as a technology forecaster, a science columnist (notably for "Science Fiction Review"), and a coordinator for computer exhibits at events like the Global Village conference at Evergreen State College. He remains active as a writer and blogger, discussing topics ranging from nanotechnology and artificial intelligence to mythology and sci-fi.

results.possibleIdentity: James Michael Sahlstrand (1936–2009) was a nationally recognized photographer and educator who spent over 30 years as a professor of art at Central Washington University (CWU) in Ellensburg, Washington. He earned his Bachelor of Arts and Master of Fine Arts from the University of Minnesota. Sahlstrand is best known for developing and curating "New Photographics," an international annual photography exhibition at the Sarah Spurgeon Gallery that ran from the early 1970s through the late 1980s and was known for its focus on innovative and experimental techniques. His personal photographic work often explored the landscape of the Pacific Northwest, characterized by a dynamic interplay of light, shadow, and color. He was closely associated with the Pacific Northwest art scene and is documented in the Jack Eyerly collection on Pacific Northwest Art, which also includes records of the Portland chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Sahlstrand was also involved with the Northwest Artists Workshop (NWAW) in the late 1970s. He was married to the artist and educator Margaret Ahrens Sahlstrand, with whom he collaborated on various artistic endeavors, including the construction of geodesic domes for their studio and home.
